sections.summary: Document title and amendment history (adopted 2017; amended 2021, 2022) with a 2022 amendment table showing revised text for labelling of non‑retail containers replaced by a reference to CXS 346-2021.
Applies the standard to cumin offered for direct consumption, as an ingredient, or for repackaging, and explicitly excludes cumin intended for industrial processing.

Defines cumin as the product from seeds of Cuminum cyminum L. processed by cleaning, drying, grinding and sifting, lists permitted styles (whole/intact, cracked, ground) and states varietal types are not applicable.
Defines the product: cumin prepared from seeds of Cuminum cyminum L. that have reached appropriate development for processing and have been suitably cleaned, dried, ground and sifted.
Specifies that cumin may be offered in three styles: whole/intact; cracked (broken into two or more pieces); or ground (powder).
Specifies that cumin may be offered in three styles: whole/intact; cracked (broken into two or more pieces); or ground (powder).
Varietal types: Not applicable (no varietal types are defined).

States composition refers to the product definition in Section 2 and sets key quality factors: maximum moisture (≤10%), characteristic odour/flavour/colour specifications, and classification into three grades with physical and chemical requirements in Tables 1 and 2; it also defines defectives and lot-acceptance rules based on sampling plans and sample averages.
Composition is defined by reference to the product definition in Section 2.

Sets quality factors for cumin including a maximum moisture content (≤10%), requirement for characteristic aroma/flavour/colour and freedom from foreign odours (especially mustiness), and provides classification into three grades with physical (Table 1) and chemical (Table 2) requirements, with ungraded product meeting at least grade III standards.
Moisture limit: cumin (whole, cracked or ground) must not contain more than 10 percent moisture.

Sensory requirements: cumin shall have a characteristic aroma and flavour (which may vary with geo‑climatic conditions), be free from foreign odour or flavour—especially mustiness—and have a characteristic colour varying from light grey to dark brown.

Classification: whole cumin may be graded into three classes (I, II, III) according to the physical and chemical requirements in Tables 1 and 2, and when ungraded the provisions for grade III apply as the minimum.

Physical requirements (Table 1) set maximum tolerances by grade for extraneous vegetable matter (I/II/III: 1/2/3 %), foreign matter (0.1/0.5/0.5 %), visible mould (1.0 % for all grades), damaged/defective fruits (5.0 %), dead insects/rodent fragments (0.1/0.5/0.5 %), insect‑damaged matter (0.5/1.0/1.0 %), prohibit live insects (0 for all grades), and limit mammalian excreta to 1.0 mg/kg.

Chemical requirements (Table 2) specify maxima and minima by grade and form, e.g. total ash (dry basis) max for whole/cracked cumin: Grade I 8.5%, II 10.0%, III 12.0% (ground 9.5%); acid‑insoluble ash max: I 1.5%, II 3.0%, III 4.0% (ground 1.5%); volatile oils (ml/100 g dry basis) minimum: I 2.0, II 1.5, III 1.5 (ground 1.3).
Definition of defectives: any lot sample failing to meet one or more applicable quality requirements in Section 3.2 (excluding those based on sample averages) shall be considered defective.

Lot acceptance: a lot meets the quality requirements if the number of defectives (per Section 3.3) does not exceed the acceptance number of the applicable sampling plan, and for factors evaluated by sample average the lot is acceptable if the average meets the tolerance and no individual sample is excessively out of tolerance.
Food additives: anticaking agents listed in Table 3 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192‑1995) may be permitted for use in ground cumin only.

Contaminants: products must comply with the maximum levels in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995) and with Codex‑established maximum residue limits for pesticides.

Food hygiene: recommends preparation and handling in accordance with the Code of Hygienic Practice for Low‑Moisture Foods (CXC 75‑2015) and other relevant Codex texts, and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.
Weights and measures: containers shall be as full as practicable without impairing quality and consistent with a proper declaration of contents.

Labelling: prepackaged products must comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985) and specifically be named “cumin” (may include style/grade), with country of origin and inspection mark optional; labelling of non‑retail containers should follow CXS 346‑2021.
Methods of analysis and sampling: compliance checking must use the relevant methods in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234‑1999).
